posts.title: Why this site exists
posts.excerpt: Two careers, one domain. A short note on why a working musician who builds websites finally built his own.
posts.body:
For over twenty years, Jon Wolf has built websites for other people through [Live Web Studios](https://livewebstudios.com). In that same stretch he's been on stage constantly, playing keys and guitar with [The British Invasion Years](https://britishinvasionyears.com) and beyond.

Those two lives mostly stayed in separate folders. This site is where they finally share a directory.

## What lives here

Over time, this is where the two worlds get written down:

- **Gear and the live rig** — running Apple MainStage on an M4 Pro MacBook, plus the Korg Kronos and Nord Electro 5 for regional dates.
- **The web side** — notes from two decades of building, hosting, and ranking sites.
- **AI for real work** — what's actually paying off as Jon builds AI workflows into everyday business processes.

## Built the way it's sold

The site itself runs on Astro, deploys on Netlify, lives in version control on GitHub, and was built with Claude in the loop — the same stack Jon would recommend to a client. Practice what you sell.

If any of that overlaps with your world — a show, a website, an AI question — [reach out](/contact/).
posts.imageAlt: A musician's desk at night with a piano keyboard beside a laptop, amber lamp and blue screen glow mixing

posts.title: The fly-in rig: trusting a laptop with the whole gig
posts.excerpt: How a backpack-sized MainStage rig replaced the road cases — the libraries that carry the show, and the habits that keep a software rig honest.
posts.body:
Every keyboard player who flies to gigs knows the moment: standing at oversized baggage, watching a case roll out, wondering what's come loose inside. A few years of that and the math gets simple — the rig that fits in a backpack wins, as long as you can trust it.

Jon's fly-in rig is **Apple MainStage** on an **M4 Pro MacBook Pro with 48GB of RAM**. The heavy lifting comes from three libraries:

- **Synthogy Ivory Upright** — the piano
- **EastWest Hollywood Opus Edition** — orchestral colors
- **NI Komplete 15** — everything else a sixties catalog can throw at a player

## A software rig is only as good as the discipline behind it

**One concert file per show, organized by setlist.** Every song is a patch, every section that needs a different sound is a patch change. No hunting, no menu-diving under stage lights.

**Headroom is reliability.** 48GB isn't a brag — it keeps every sampled instrument resident in memory, so there's no disk-streaming surprise mid-chorus. RAM is the cheapest insurance a software rig can buy.

**Rehearse the failure.** Before every run, force-quit MainStage and time the recovery: reboot, reopen, ready. Knowing it's under two minutes changes how you breathe when something flickers.

**Freeze the machine.** The gig laptop gets no OS updates, no new plugins, no experiments during a season. Updates happen in the off weeks, then get soundchecked before they're trusted.

## When the hardware still rides along

For regional dates within a few hours' drive of North Jersey, the **Korg Kronos** and **Nord Electro 5** come along — they don't need a recovery plan. But when the gig is a flight away, the backpack rig has earned the seat.
posts.imageAlt: A laptop and compact MIDI controller packed in an open backpack on a dark stage floor under amber light

posts.title: Four voices, one chart: the harmony work nobody sees
posts.excerpt: The British Invasion era lives and dies on vocal harmony. A look at the charts that keep a touring tribute locked to the records.
posts.body:
Ask anyone what makes a Beatles song a Beatles song and they'll talk about the melodies. But play one with a band and the truth shows up fast: the magic is in the stack. Those records are vocal-harmony machines — thirds that split and rejoin, unison lines that bloom into triads exactly one beat before the hook.

In [The British Invasion Years](https://britishinvasionyears.com), Jon assists in laying out the multi-voice harmony charts — helping write them, maintain them, and update them when arrangements shift. It's the least visible job in the show and one of the most consequential.

## What the work comes down to

- **Transcribe the record, not the memory.** Everyone *thinks* they know the harmony to these songs. The actual voicings are frequently stranger — and better — than the version in anyone's head. The chart settles arguments before they start.
- **Write for the singers you have.** A chart that's correct but sits in the wrong part of a singer's range gets quietly ignored by the second show. Voice assignments matter as much as the notes.
- **Notate the breaths.** When four people phrase a line differently, it sounds like four people. When they breathe in the same places, it sounds like a record.
- **Treat charts as maintenance, not monuments.** Keys change, personnel change, an arrangement picks up a new tag for the live show. A binder of stale charts is worse than none, because people trust it.

## Why it matters

It's arranging as quality control. The audience never sees a chart — they just hear a chorus lock into place and feel, for three minutes, like it's 1964. That feeling is the whole job.
posts.imageAlt: Vocal harmony charts on a music stand under warm stage light with vintage microphones behind

posts.title: Where AI actually fits in a small business (and where it doesn't)
posts.excerpt: After twenty years building websites for small businesses, an honest map of where AI earns its keep — and the three places it quietly fails.
posts.body:
Two decades at [Live Web Studios](https://livewebstudios.com) is a long time to watch small businesses adopt technology. The pattern never changes: a wave of hype, a rush of bad purchases, then a quiet settling into the two or three uses that actually matter. AI is following the same script — so here's a map drawn from the settling, not the hype.

## Where it earns its keep

- **The writing nobody wants to do.** Follow-up emails, service descriptions, FAQ answers, job postings. Not the brand voice on the homepage — the workaday text that's been sitting half-finished for months. AI gets a draft in front of you in seconds, and editing a draft beats facing a blank page.
- **First-pass triage.** Sorting inbound email by intent, summarizing a long thread before a call, pulling action items out of meeting notes. Anywhere a human currently skims so a decision can happen, AI skims well.
- **Institutional memory.** Feed price sheets, policies, and process docs into a well-built assistant and new staff stop interrupting your best person eleven times a day. Not glamorous; enormous payoff.

## Where it quietly fails

- **Anything customer-facing without a human valve.** An AI answering phones or reviews unsupervised is a brand wearing a blindfold. Draft, then approve. Always.
- **Math you'd bet the business on.** Quotes, payroll, margins. Language models are persuasive and confidently wrong — the wrong combination for arithmetic.
- **Replacing judgment that was never written down.** If a process lives entirely in someone's head, there's nothing to automate yet. Documenting it comes first — and that work pays off even if it's never automated.

## The pattern that works

The businesses getting real value aren't using AI everywhere. They're using it in three boring places, consistently, with a human holding the last word. Finding those three places is exactly the conversation Jon is having these days — [reach out](/contact/).
posts.imageAlt: A small business owner at a wooden shop counter at dusk reviewing a laptop

posts.title: Does a small business still need a website in 2026?
posts.excerpt: Social media, map listings, AI answers — with all of it, does a small business still need its own site? Short answer: more than ever.
posts.body:
It's a fair question in 2026. Customers find businesses through map listings, social feeds, and AI assistants that answer before anyone clicks. So does a small business still need its own website?

Yes — and arguably more than ever. Here's the plain-English case.

## A website is the only space you own

Everything else is rented:

- **Social platforms** change their rules, their reach, and sometimes their existence.
- **Map and directory listings** put competitors one tap away.
- **AI assistants** summarize businesses using whatever they can find — and a real site is the best source to find.

A website is the one address that's entirely yours, that nobody can throttle or shut off.

## What a small-business site actually has to do

Forget the brochure mindset. A site that pulls its weight will:

- **Load fast** — most visitors leave a slow page before it finishes.
- **Answer the top three questions** a customer has before they call.
- **Make contact one tap away** — phone, directions, a short form.
- **Feed the other channels** — clean information for Google, AI tools, and anyone sharing a link.

## The 2026 wrinkle: AI reads your site too

AI assistants increasingly answer "who should I call for X near me" by reading the open web. Clear, well-structured pages — real headings, real text, sensible markup — are what those systems quote. A thin or broken site simply gets skipped.

A good website in 2026 isn't a digital brochure. It's the source of truth every other channel pulls from. That's the work [Live Web Studios](https://livewebstudios.com) has done since 2004 — and [Jon is glad to talk it through](/contact/).
posts.imageAlt: A small storefront at blue hour with a glowing smartphone held in the foreground

posts.title: Local SEO for New Jersey businesses: a plain-English guide
posts.excerpt: Getting found by neighbors searching for what you sell. The handful of local-SEO moves that actually move the needle for NJ businesses.
posts.body:
"Local SEO" sounds like jargon, but the goal is simple: when someone nearby searches for what you sell, you show up. For a New Jersey business competing with the whole tri-state area, that visibility is the difference between a busy week and a quiet one.

Here's what actually moves the needle — minus the jargon.

## Start with the basics that big tools ignore

- **Claim and complete your Google Business Profile.** Accurate hours, categories, photos, and service area. This is the single highest-leverage local move.
- **Keep your name, address, and phone identical everywhere.** Site, profile, directories, social. Inconsistencies quietly hurt rankings.
- **Earn real reviews, then reply to them.** Volume, recency, and responses all matter — and they're the first thing a human reads anyway.

## Make the website say where you are

- **Name the towns you serve** in real sentences, not a stuffed footer list.
- **Give each location or service its own page** if you have several — one thin page can't rank for everything.
- **Add local structured data** so search engines can read your address, hours, and area cleanly.

## Then earn the local signals

- **Get listed where your community actually looks** — chambers of commerce, local press, regional directories.
- **Publish the occasional local-interest piece** tied to your work and your area.

None of this is a trick. Local SEO rewards a business that is genuinely easy to find, easy to trust, and clear about where it works. [Live Web Studios](https://livewebstudios.com) has been doing exactly that for New Jersey businesses since 2004.
posts.imageAlt: A small-town New Jersey main street at golden hour with brick storefronts

posts.title: Booking live music for a private event: a quick guide
posts.excerpt: Hiring a pianist or band for a wedding, party, or corporate night? A working musician's short list of what to ask before you book.
posts.body:
Hiring live music for a wedding, milestone party, or corporate evening is one of those decisions that quietly makes or breaks the night. As someone on the other side of the booking — Jon plays solo piano and performs with a full touring band — here's the short list worth running through before signing anything.

## Match the act to the room

- **Solo piano** suits cocktail hours, dinners, ceremonies, and anywhere conversation matters.
- **A full band** suits dancing, a big reveal, or a crowd that wants a show.
- Some events want both across the night — ask whether the act can scale.

## Questions worth asking up front

- **Can they read the room?** Volume and song choice should flex with the moment, not run a fixed setlist.
- **What does the setup need?** Power, space, and load-in access are easy to overlook until the day of.
- **Do they take requests** — and is there a window to send must-plays and do-not-plays in advance?
- **What's the backup plan** if gear fails? A pro has one.

## What a professional brings beyond the playing

- A rig that's reliable, not just impressive.
- Appropriate dress and a low-drama presence.
- Clear communication before the date, so nothing's a surprise.

The right music disappears into the evening in the best way — it just feels like the night was always going to sound that good. [Booking a date? Reach out.](/contact/)
posts.imageAlt: A grand piano in an elegant ballroom with string lights and an empty dance floor

posts.title: The keyboards that made the British Invasion — and how they fit in a backpack now
posts.excerpt: Vox Continental, Hohner Pianet, Mellotron, Hammond — the four keyboards that defined the era, and what it takes to carry all of them to a gig in 2026.
posts.body:
Strip the guitars off the British Invasion records and you'll find a second band underneath — a small museum of keyboards that defined how the era *felt*. Playing keys in [The British Invasion Years](https://britishinvasionyears.com) means being curator of that museum every night. Four instruments do most of the work.

## The big four

- **Vox Continental.** The reedy, top-boosted organ of the era. John Lennon played one at Shea Stadium on "I'm Down"; The Animals rode one through "House of the Rising Sun." Nothing else sits in a mix quite like it.
- **Hohner Pianet.** The bitey little electric piano under "The Night Before" and The Zombies' "She's Not There." Often mistaken for a Wurlitzer; the attack is harder, almost plucked.
- **Mellotron.** Tape loops under every key — the haunted flutes that open "Strawberry Fields Forever." The original sampler, with all the wobble that implies.
- **Hammond organ.** By 1967 the British bands had discovered what American soul already knew. "A Whiter Shade of Pale" is the era saying goodbye through a Leslie cabinet.

## Carrying the museum

The originals are heavy, temperamental, and increasingly precious — a touring tribute can't gamble a show on a fifty-year-old tape frame. So the museum travels as software: Arturia's Mellotron, the organ and EP collections inside NI Komplete 15, all hosted in Apple MainStage on the fly-in rig.

The trick isn't owning the sounds. It's *playing* them honestly:

- **Match the touch, not just the patch.** A Pianet line played with piano technique sounds wrong even with a perfect sample. Shorter attacks, flatter dynamics.
- **Keep the flaws.** The Mellotron's pitch drift and the Vox's thin bass are the character. Cleaning them up kills the time machine.
- **Know which song needs which board.** The audience may not know a Continental from a Hammond by name — but they absolutely hear when "96 Tears"-style reediness shows up where church-organ warmth belongs.

Get those right and a 2026 laptop disappears, and it's 1965 in the room again. That disappearing act is the whole gig.
posts.imageAlt: A vintage 1960s combo organ and electric piano on a dark stage under a tungsten spotlight

posts.title: How a setlist gets built: pacing a two-hour tribute night
posts.excerpt: A two-hour show is an argument with the audience's energy, settled in advance. How a touring tribute decides what gets played, where, and why.
posts.body:
People imagine a setlist is a stack of favorite songs in a pleasing order. It's closer to architecture: a two-hour structure that has to hold the weight of a room's attention without a sag in the middle.

In [The British Invasion Years](https://britishinvasionyears.com), the set is built, argued over, and rebuilt constantly. The principles stay the same.

## Open with recognition, not the best song

The first ninety seconds buy the next two hours. The opener's job is instant recognition — the song a casual fan knows by the second bar. The *best* material lands harder twenty minutes in, after the room has decided to trust you.

## Think in blocks, not songs

- **Energy blocks** — three or four up-tempo numbers that build momentum and let the dance floor commit.
- **The breather** — a ballad placed where the room needs it, not where the chronology says. Tired feet make grateful listeners.
- **The history thread** — the show moves loosely through the era, early Merseybeat toward the psychedelic turn, so the night tells a story without a lecture.

## Protect the singers

A set that looks great on paper can be brutal on voices — four harmony-heavy numbers in a row will sand the top off any tenor by the encore. Vocal load gets spaced the way a good chart spaces breaths.

## Leave room to call an audible

Two or three slots stay flexible every night. A room that wants to dance gets another mover; a listening room gets the deep cut. The printed list is a plan, not a contract.

## End past the ending

The encore isn't extra — it's the part people hum in the parking lot. The closer before it should feel final, so the return feels like a gift instead of a schedule.

None of this is visible from the floor. That's the point: when the pacing is right, the night just feels inevitable.
posts.imageAlt: A printed setlist taped to a dark stage floor beside guitar pedals under a single spotlight

posts.title: What twenty years of redesigns taught me about websites that last
posts.excerpt: After two decades of building and rebuilding sites at Live Web Studios, the patterns are clear: what survives every redesign, and what gets thrown out every time.
posts.body:
Run a web shop long enough and you stop seeing websites as launches. You see them as generations — because at [Live Web Studios](https://livewebstudios.com), plenty of client sites have been rebuilt three or four times since 2004. Watching what survives each rebuild and what gets thrown away is its own education.

## What gets thrown out every time

- **The clever stuff.** Flash intros, carousels, parallax everything, whatever widget was fashionable. Every generation's decoration is the next generation's demolition.
- **Design built around a trend instead of the business.** If the look came from an awards gallery instead of the customer's question, it ages in dog years.
- **Anything that requires a manual.** Features the owner never learned to update die quietly, then get deleted loudly.

## What survives every rebuild

- **The words.** Clear descriptions of what the business does, for whom, at what service area — written once, polished for years. Content outlives every coat of paint.
- **The domain and its history.** Age, links, and a clean record are compound interest. (It's also why moving hosts carelessly is so expensive.)
- **The phone number placement.** Every redesign, the contact path gets one tap shorter. It never gets longer.
- **Speed.** The fast site survives; the heavy one gets rebuilt. No exception in twenty years.

## The musician's version

It's the same lesson the stage teaches: arrangements change, the song remains. A redesign should be a new arrangement of material that was worth keeping — and if the material isn't worth keeping, the redesign isn't the problem.

The sites that last aren't the ones that impressed other designers at launch. They're the ones that kept answering the customer's question, generation after generation.
posts.imageAlt: A workbench covered in website design sketches beside an open laptop under a warm desk lamp

posts.title: Your band needs a website more than another demo
posts.excerpt: Talent buyers spend ninety seconds deciding. A working musician's case for why a band site beats one more recording — and what belongs on it.
posts.body:
Here's an uncomfortable truth from both sides of the booking table: most gigs are won or lost before anyone presses play. A talent buyer with forty acts to consider spends about ninety seconds per band — and that minute and a half happens on the web.

Jon lives this from both directions: performing with [The British Invasion Years](https://britishinvasionyears.com) and building sites for working bands through [Live Band Web Studios](https://livebandwebstudios.com). The pattern is consistent.

## What a buyer actually needs to see

- **Live video above everything.** Not the studio single — proof the band delivers in a room. Two minutes of a real crowd reacting beats a produced EPK every time.
- **A current date list.** Working bands work. A shows page that's six months stale whispers that the band might be too.
- **One paragraph that says what you are.** "A touring tribute to the Beatles and the British Invasion era" closes more inquiries than three hundred words of biography.
- **Contact without friction.** The buyer is deciding between you and a band whose phone number was easier to find.

## What quietly kills inquiries

- A social-media-only presence. Algorithms decide who sees a post; nobody algorithm-gates your own site.
- Music that auto-plays. The buyer is at a desk, probably on a call.
- A dead link anywhere in the first ninety seconds.

## The demo paradox

Another recording improves the product. The website improves the *odds anyone hears the product*. Most bands have the ratio backwards — five hundred dollars of studio time for a band with no working shows page is a great song nobody books.

The bands working most aren't always the best ones. They're the easiest ones to say yes to.
posts.imageAlt: An open laptop on a backstage road case glowing with a band website, stage lights blurred behind

posts.title: What makes a great British Invasion tribute show
posts.excerpt: Anyone can play the hits. A tribute that actually transports a room gets a few quieter things right — from a player on the inside.
posts.body:
Plenty of bands can play sixties hits. A British Invasion tribute that actually transports a room is doing several quieter things right — and from inside [The British Invasion Years](https://britishinvasionyears.com), the difference is clear.

## The harmonies have to be real

The British Invasion era is a vocal-harmony era. Get the stack right and a chorus lifts the whole room; fake it and the song feels like a cover. That means actual charts, actual voice assignments, and singers who breathe together — not three people guessing at the same melody.

## The sounds have to be period-correct

- **Keyboards** that capture the real combo organ, electric piano, and early synth voices — not a generic patch.
- **Guitar tones** that sit in the sixties, not a modern high-gain wall.
- **Arrangements** faithful to the records, with the little signature parts intact.

## The show has to be paced

- A set is built, not shuffled — energy that rises, breathes, and lands.
- The talk between songs sets the era without turning into a lecture.
- The deep cuts are chosen to reward the fans without losing everyone else.

## The musicianship has to be invisible

The highest compliment a tribute can earn is that the audience stops thinking about the band and starts remembering where they were in 1965. That takes serious players who are happy to disappear into the songs.

That's the bar the show aims at every night. [Booking a show? Reach out.](/contact/)
posts.imageAlt: Silhouettes of a four-piece band in sixties suits backlit in amber and red stage light
